entry.title: SAP BTP - APIM - Domain-Centric Routing Pattern (DCRP): Governing APIs via CPI Provider - Part I
entry.content:
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

The Problem: I've encountered this scenario across some projects—API proxy sprawl creating versioning chaos and maintenance nightmares. Deploy one proxy, break three others. Sound familiar?

The Solution: DCRP (Domain-Centric Routing Pattern)—a metadata-driven governance framework that consolidates multiple backend services into one business-domain-oriented API Proxy.

The Proof: 4 business domains deployed in 8 minutes. 8 iFlows managed by 4 API Proxies (not 8 separate proxies). 1 JavaScript routing engine (reused across all domains). 96% deployment time reduction vs 1:1 proxy model.

The Impact: Add new iFlow in 30 seconds (KVM update only). 96% deployment time reduction vs traditional 1:1 proxy model. Unified monitoring, versioning, and security per domain.

Who Should Read: Integration Platform Owners, Enterprise Architects, SAP BTP Strategy Managers, Integration Consultants, APIM & CPI Experts, and anyone in the integration community looking for scalable governance solutions

Time Investment: Executive Summary (1 minute). Full Article (25-30 minutes technical deep dive).

Additional Resources: PDF-Slide deck and presentation purposes.

  • EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
  • The DCRP (Domain-Centric Routing Pattern) : Solving Proxy Sprawl at Enterprise Scale
  • Introduction
  • 1 - The "Sprawl" Problem - An Overview
  • 2 - What is DCRP (Domain Centric Routing Pattern)?
  • 2.2 - DCRP Decision Framework
  • 3 - The API Proxy Design Pattern provides:
  • 4 - Implementation: Configuring the DCRP API Proxy
  • 4.4 - Target Endpoint Configuration
  • 5 - The Semantic Facade: One Proxy, Multiple Intentions
  • 6 - The Semantic Bridge: Public API to Technical Endpoints
  • 7 - The Routing Engine: Understanding the API-Proxy Execution Flow
  • 7.2 - Why This Sequence Matters for DCRP
  • 7.3 - The DCRP Brain executes 4 policy stages:
  • 8 - The Brain Components: Key Value Mapping and JavaScript Logic
  • 8.1 - Key-Value Map (KVM): The Memory
  • 8.2 - Scaling the Pattern: Two Levels of High-Scale Flexibility
  • IMPORTANT: iFlow Endpoint Prerequisite ( in case of imidiatly test )
  • 8.6 - The Template Philosophy: Scaling Strategies
  • 8.7 - When Domain Templates Win:
  • Break-even point:5 instances per domain
  • 9 - The Domain Template Contract:
  • 9.1 - Deployment Checklist (Domain Template Strategy):
  • 9.2 JavaScript Policy – Dissecting the DCRP Routing Engine
  • 9.3- The Complete JavaScript Code
  • 9.4 - How the Routing Engine Works
  • 9.5 - Chapter 9 Summary
  • 10 -Statistics Collector – Capturing Business Intelligence
  • Conclusion

The DCRP (Domain-Centric Routing Pattern) : Solving Proxy Sprawl at Enterprise Scale

The DCRP (Domain-Centric Routing Pattern) is a semantic name that I created to describe a governance methodology designed to solve "Proxy Sprawl" and "CPI Package Sprawl" in large-scale SAP Integration landscapes. Rather than being a new technical innovation, it is an architectural approach leveraging native SAP APIM dynamic routing capabilities to create a business-centric domain layer.

My goal is to bridge high-level governance with hands-on execution, to ensure clarity for all technical levels, I have manually designed visual integration diagrams and step-by-step guides to make this pattern accessible to everyone.

"Patterns are the 'things' that repeat in a design; pattern languages are the way we capture them."
Christopher Alexander

Spoiler
Disclaimer:
Target Audience
• Integration Platform Owners
• Enterprise Architects
• SAP BTP Strategy Managers
Prerequisistes:
In-depth experience with SAP BTP API Management and metadata-driven routing architectures

Introduction

The SAP's official docs tell you HOW to use dynamic routing. What they don't tell you is WHEN, WHY, or how to govern it at scale. That's the gap I'm filling here. Enable-dynamic-routing .This is where my framework comes in: a complete end-to-end solution—from architectural analysis and design to implementation and rigorous stress testing, all developed to validate the strategy I am now releasing to you.

In my last post, I threw the idea out there. Now let's go verry deep. - SAP BTP - APIM - Advanced Domain Routing API-Proxy Pattern for Governing Similar Backend Endpoints - I introduced the concepts. Now the focusing on DCRP application to a specific business domain: Sales & Distribution (SAP SD / O2C), with explanations and possibilities that can be scaled for other business processes.

What if you were able to via single API proxy receiver calls from several CRM ?

What if your API proxy was fully governed, all request traces were stored, you did not have to worry about versioning, and you had high scalability ?

In this article, I will provide a hypothetical yet realistic example of how 3 different CRMs—Microsoft D365, Salesforce, and a Vendor CRM—could integrate into the sales business process domain. I will provide step-by-step guidance on how to develop this Domain-Centric Routing Pattern (DCRP) API-Proxy.

In the next blog - Part II - I'll cover SAP CPI implementation proposing to solve a common issue I've observed in many projects: what I call 'CPI Package Sprawl' and in the Part III, testing and monitoring. I separated these intentionally because combining them all would create another SAP Press Book, not a blog.

In the sections that follow, I'll walk you through the business context, architectural rationale, and hands-on implementation—ensuring you have everything needed to apply DCRP in your own landscape.

Figure 1 - DCRP - API Proxy Model detailed exploration

1 - The "Sprawl" Problem - An Overview

What I've called "proxy sprawl", I have seen many times in practice: an environment that has one-to-one API Proxies for each Service, even though there may be no relation to their respective Business Domains.

Without some form of Central Governance Authority or naming convention(s), environments become Un-Governed and ultimately Un-Sustainable.

Here's what actually happens in the field:

  • URLs become a mess - /ProcurementOrderService next to /SIEMENS-MANUFACTURING-V1. No one knows who created what.
  • Versioning ? Forget it - Is this v2.1 or v2.1-hotfix-final-REALLY-FINAL? Nobody knows until something breaks.
  • Security theater - Half these proxies are glorified pass-throughs with zero rate limiting. You're one DDoS away from a very bad Monday.
  • Monitoring black hole - When something fails, good luck figuring out if it's the backend, the proxy, or just bad client data.

This is not hypothetical. DCRP was developed from resolving these issues directly in my own Development Work. Through rigorous testing, I formally defined this methodology as a solution to share with the SAP Integration Community.

Note: The following image is a mere conceptual illustration to help visualize the issue. Actual enterprise architectures typically involve much greater scale and complexity.

Figure 2 - The Sprawl Problem 

 

2 - What is DCRP (Domain Centric Routing Pattern)?

DCRP (Domain Centric Routing Pattern), is an Architectural Design Pattern that unifies multiple backend services into a single Business-Domain-Oriented API Proxy. DCRP resides at the Gateway Layer, controlling which aspects of the backend services are made available and how they will be routed, all without affecting how the backend service is exposed using either REST or SOAP protocols.

Rather than organize APIs by technical silos, DCRP organizes them based on the business domains of the organization, which helps reduce fragmentation of the APIs while still being fully compatible with SAP's API Product offerings, Policies, and Security Models.

2.1 - The Three Pillars of DCRP
  • Metadata Driven Execution: Routing logic lives in KVM, not code. Change metadata, change behavior—zero deployment
  • Secure Whitelisting: Rejects unauthorized requests and prevents unintentional exposure of endpoints through explicit path whitelisting. The gateway remains as a controlled facade.
  • Architectural Resilience: DCRP can absorb changes to the backend through metadata updates, thus providing a stable interface for consumers of the api and encapsulating versioning complexities within the gateway layer.

2.2 - DCRP Decision Framework

2.2.1 - Use DCRP when:
  • All endpoints belong to the same business domain
  • Services represent variants of the same business process
  • Backend integration flows share the same CPI package
  • API contracts, versioning, and lifecycle are unified
  • Single team ownership across all services
  • Need for consolidated monitoring and analytics
2.2.2 - Do not use DCRP when:
  • Different business domains are mixed (Sales + Logistics + Finance)
  • Independent versioning strategies are required
  • Multiple teams with independent lifecycles
  • Radically different security or SLA requirements
  • Legal/compliance requires physical endpoint separation
  • Only 1-2 services per domain (overhead exceeds benefit)

Now that we understand what DCRP is and its foundational pillars, let's visualize how this pattern operates in a real integration scenario

Figure 3 - DCRP Architecture Overview end-to-end: Three-CRM Integration Scenario

3 - The API Proxy Design Pattern provides:

DCRP leverages SAP APIM's native Proxy Endpoint and Target Endpoint architecture. The Proxy Endpoint acts as the intelligent router (handling authentication, rate limiting, and security), while the Target Endpoint serves as the dynamic connector (resolving backend destinations at runtime via metadata).

  • Key principle: The Proxy Endpoint handles business logic (who is calling and where should they go), while the Target Endpoint handles technical connectivity (how to communicate with each specific backend).
  • Policy Enforcement: Centralized security, quotas, throttling (Spike Arrest), custom logic (JavaScript), and analytics collection reside at the gateway layer. This article focuses on JavaScript routing logic and analytics; extensive materials on other policy types are available in the SAP Community.

Figure 4 - API Proxy Pattern Architecture: Request/Response Flow through Gateway Layer

4 - Implementation: Configuring the DCRP API Proxy

In this section, we will define the entry the configuration details of ProxyEndpoint property, routing rules and target endpoint properties.

4.1 - The Entry Contract: One API Proxy per Business Domain

To ensure a predictable and governed environment, DCRP eliminates proxy sprawl by defining the entry contract based on business domain rather than technical application. Each business domain has exactly one API Proxy.

4.2 - Naming Convention Strategy

A consistent naming convention provides immediate contextual information to any architect reviewing the API portal.

The structure suggestion follows this pattern:

[Provider]-[Pattern]-[Domain]-[Sub-Domain]-[Protocol]-api

Example of Our Proxy Names:

cpi-dcrp-sales-orders-odata-api

  • cpi: Identifies the API provider (SAP Cloud Integration Suite).
  • dcrp: Defines the routing methodology used.
  • sales-orders: Identifies the business domain of the process (O2C).
  • odata: Represents the primary protocol of the contract.
4.3 - API Proxy Configuration

API Proxy Details:

  • Name: cpi-dcrp-sales-orders-odata-api
  • Title: The api-proxy-adrcp-sales-process
  • Type: REST
  • Base URL: https://.apimanagement.eu20.hana.ondemand.com/sales

4.4 - Target Endpoint Configuration

Target Endpoint Properties:

Property
  • Name: cpi.host
  • Value: https://.cfapps.eu20.hana.ondemand.com
Proxy Endpoint Properties:
  • Name:backend.system
  • Value: sap-cpi

Routing Rules:

  • Target Endpoint: sap-cpi-api-endpoints
  • Route Rule: default
  • Condition: proxy.pathsuffix MatchesPath "/orders/**"

Target Endpoint Configuration
  • Name: cpi.host
  • Value: https:// <sapcpiaddress>.cfapps.eu20.hana.ondemand.com
  • Name: cpi.basepath
  • Value: /http/orders  <- httppath endpoint from cpi API

5 - The Semantic Facade: One Proxy, Multiple Intentions

With the Proxy and Target configuration set, let's examine the API Contract. DCRP provides a clean interface to external consumers while handling complexity behind the scenes.

All consumers call the same API Proxy using semantic paths, with routing differentiated via path suffix, custom headers, or correlation IDs—not separate proxies.

5.1 - Key Benefits:
  • Total Abstraction: If you migrate an iFlow or move to a native S/4HANA OData service in the future, the consumer's entry contract remains untouched.
  • End-to-End Monitoring: A single proxy enables consolidated analytics via the Statistics Collector, making it easier to monitor the entire Sales domain in one view.
  • Centralized Security Policies: Apply a single OAuth2, API Key or Basic Auth policy—plus unified Spike Arrest and Quotas based on X-SenderId or X-CorrelationId headers, xml and json size restriction - for the entire Sales domain.

Note: This article focuses on routing logic and analytics. For comprehensive coverage of security policies (OAuth2, API Keys, Spike Arrest, Quotas, Threat Protection, SQL Injection prevention), refer to SAP Community Network security best practices documentation.

6 - The Semantic Bridge: Public API to Technical Endpoints

This mapping demonstrates how DCRP abstracts technical complexity by providing a unified, clean interface for consumers. The Gateway translates these semantic paths into specific backend iFlows dynamically at runtime.

Public API Endpoint ( Consumer-Facing)Backend CPI Endpoint ( Technical ) 
https://<apim>/sales/orders/*Base routing pattern (Sales Order Domain simulation)
https://<apim>/sales/orders/inboundhttps://<cpi>/http/orders/inbound
https://<apim>/sales/orders/trackinghttps://<cpi>/http/orders/tracking
https://<apim>/sales/orders/historyhttps://<cpi>/http/orders/history
https://<apim>/sales/orders/detailhttps://<cpi>/http/orders/detail
https://<apim>/sales/orders/returnshttps://<cpi>/http/order/returns
https://<apim>/sales/orders/statushttps://<cpi>/http/order/status
6.1 - Key observations:
  • Single Entry Point: All consumers call the same base URL (https://<apim>/sales/orders/*)
  • Path-Based Routing: The path suffix (/inbound, /tracking, etc.) determines the target CPI iFlow
  • Consumer Abstraction: External systems never know or care about the CPI technical endpoints
  • Backend Flexibility: You can change CPI URLs without impacting consumer contracts
6.2 – GET request examples: Maintaining HTTP Protocol Integrity

DCRP preserves path parameters and query strings as Camel Headers within SAP CPI.

Example: Complex retrieval with path parameter and query string

  • Public API Call: GET https://apim/sales/orders/tracking/INV-2024-001?include=shipments
  • Technical Target CPI URL: https://cpi/http/orders/tracking/INV-2024-001?include=shipments
  • Result in CPI: CamelHttpPath = /INV-2024-001 CamelHttpQuery = include=shipments

6.3 - Key Takeaway:

In traditional 1:1 proxy models, path and query parameter handling is straightforward—the gateway forwards requests to a static backend URL. With DCRP, however, the final target URL is dynamically constructed through the combination of Key-Value Map metadata and JavaScript policy logic—the "brain" of the routing engine.

The scenarios above serve as illustrative guidance to show what happens from the consumer's perspective. The actual magic—how the JavaScript dynamically constructs these URLs at runtime—will be revealed in the routing engine section below.

7 - The Routing Engine: Understanding the API-Proxy Execution Flow

Before we dive into the JavaScript "brain," we need to understand how the SAP API Management Proxy Design Pattern works internally. The routing magic doesn't happen in isolation—it's part of a precise execution sequence.

7.1 - The Three-Stage Execution Flow

Every request that enters an API Proxy goes through three critical stages:

7.1.1 - Stage 1: Proxy Endpoint Pre-Flow 

This is where the request first arrives. At this stage, the gateway executes:

  • Security policies (OAuth2, API Key validation, Basic Auth)
  • Request validation (payload size, content type, etc.)
  • Traffic control policies (Spike Arrest, Quotas)

If any policy fails here, the request is rejected immediately—it never reaches the backend.

7.1.2 - Stage 2: Routing Rules 

After all Proxy Endpoint policies pass successfully, the gateway evaluates the Routing Rules to determine which Target Endpoint should handle the request.

Example routing rule:

Condition: proxy.pathsuffix MatchesPath "/orders/**"

This acts as a gatekeeper: only requests matching /orders/** are forwarded to the configured Target Endpoint. Invalid paths are rejected with HTTP 404.

7.1.3 - Stage 3: Target Endpoint Pre-Flow 

This is where the magic happens. At this stage:

  • The request has been authenticated and crossed the ProxyEndpoint - Pre-Flow
  • The routing rule has been validated means the header and runtime variables keep intact
  • The gateway is about to connect to the backend

7.2 - Why This Sequence Matters for DCRP

If any policy fails during the Proxy Endpoint Pre-Flow processing, a fault is raised immediately—the request never reaches the dynamic routing mechanism or CPI. However, when all policies pass successfully and the path condition configured within the Proxy Endpoint matches (e.g., proxy.pathsuffix MatchesPath "/orders/**"), the message flows through the internal gateway and routes to the Target Endpoint Pre-Flow. This is where the entire DCRP routing logic is applied.

Critical Point: The Target Pre-Flow is the LAST point where we can modify target.url before SAP APIM connects to the backend. This is why all DCRP routing logic resides here. The diagram below demonstrates this complete three-stage execution flow.

Figure 5 - The DCRP Brain (Viana Pattern)

7.3 - The DCRP Brain executes 4 policy stages:

  • Key/Variable extraction (httppath,iflowname,sapprocess)
  • JavaScript Routing (verification variables, validation, injection of variables and headers)
  • RaiseFaultException ( in case of any exception, miss variables, wrong configuration of VM the javascript blocks de process)
  • Statistics collection (custom dimensions variables for SAP APIM Dashboard)

8 - The Brain Components: Key Value Mapping and JavaScript Logic

Now that we understand where the routing logic operates (Target Endpoint Pre-Flow) and when it executes (after authentication and routing rule validation), let's examine the two critical components that power the DCRP Brain: the metadata storage (KVM) and the decision engine (JavaScript policy).

8.1 - Key-Value Map (KVM): The Memory

Where do we store routing metadata? SAP gives us Key-Value Maps (KVM) for exactly this. Think of it as a tiny database that lives in your API Management tenant—fast, secure, and perfect for our needs.

Important point, each SAP CPI package that fits over this pattern the package name should be created separate in KVM, the register is unic per business domain

Sample KVM Configuration:

KeyValue
packagenamenx.sales.domain.process
sapprocessSAP SD & O2C - Sales Process
iflownameinbound:SO.01.Iflow.Sales.Order.Process.Inbound,tracking:SO.02.Iflow.Sales.Order.Tracking.Provider,httppath cpi:nameiflow

Figure 6 - Key-Value Map Configuration

8.2 - Scaling the Pattern: Two Levels of High-Scale Flexibility

The DCRP pattern enables scalability at two distinct levels, both driven by metadata configuration rather than code modification.

Pattern String Structure: <httppathcpi>:<iflowname><delimiter>

Supported Delimiters: The routing engine automatically detects and supports multiple delimiters between entries: , (comma - recommended), /, \, [, ], (, ), -, ;, |, _. The colon (:) is reserved for separating path from iFlow name.

Recommended Format: Use comma (,) as the standard delimiter for consistency and clarity across teams.

8.2.1 - Level 1: Vertical Scaling (Within a Single API Proxy)

Add as many iFlows as needed within the same business domain by simply updating the iflowname string in KVM.

Example - Sales Domain KVM:

inbound:S0.01.Iflow.Sales.Order.Process.Inbound,
tracking:S0.02.Iflow.Sales.Order.Tracking.Provider,
history:S0.03.Iflow.Sales.Order.History.Provider,
detail:S0.04.Iflow.Sales.Order.Detail.Provider,
outbound:S0.05.Iflow.Sales.Order.Process.Outbound,
returns:S0.06.Iflow.Sales.Order.Returns.Handler,
quotes:S0.07.Iflow.Sales.Quote.Generator,
contracts:S0.08.Iflow.Sales.Contract.Manager,

Alternative formatting (same data, different delimiter):

inbound:SO.01.Iflow.Sales.Order.Process.Inbound |
tracking:S0.02.Iflow.Sales.Order.Tracking.Provider | 
history:S0.03.Iflow.Sales.Order.History.Provider |

Action required:

  • Update KVM entries following the pattern
  • JavaScript modification: None
  • Capacity: 100+ iFlows per business domain
8.2.2 - Level 2: Horizontal Scaling (Across Multiple Business Domains)

Replicate the pattern for new business processes by creating additional API Proxies, each with its own dedicated KVM.

Step 1: Create new KVM for the business domain

Example KVM identifiers that should be changed in the Policy:

mapIdentifier="cpipackage-nx.sales.domain.process" (Sales)
mapIdentifier="cpipackage-nx.logistics.domain.process" (Logistics)
mapIdentifier="cpipackage-nx.finance.domain.process" (Finance)

Step 2: Clone the DCRP API Proxy Template
Step 3: Update the KVM Policy XML to reference the new mapIdentifier
Step 4: Deploy

Basically: After cloning the API-Proxy, just change this parameter in the KVM Policy:

mapIdentifier="new-cpi-package-process-x"

JavaScript modification: None (same logic applies across all domains)

8.2.3 - Real-World Validation: 4 Business Domains Deployed
Business Domain API Proxy CPI Package iFlows Time
Sales & O2Ccpi-dcrp-sales-orders-odata-apicpipackage-nx.sales.domain.process22 min
Logistics & Shippingcpi-dcrp-logistics-shipments-odata-apicpipackage-nx.logistics.domain.process22 min
Finance & Billingcpi-dcrp-finance-invoices-odata-apicpipackage-nx.finance.domain.process22 min
Procurementcpi-dcrp-procurement-orders-odata-apicpipackage-nx.procurement.domain.process22 min

Results:

4 API Proxies managing 8 iFlows

JavaScript policies reused: 1 (universal routing engine)Total deployment time: ~8 minutes

Figure 7 - Before and After DCRP Implementation

Key Insights:

What This Proves:

  • Metadata-Driven Scaling — Same JavaScript code across all 4 proxies; only KVM mapIdentifier, cpi.basepath, and Routing Rules vary per domain
  • Zero Logic Changes — JavaScript routing engine, RaiseFault policy, Statistics Collector remain identical across domains
  • Deployment Breakdown:
    • Clone template: approximately 30s
    • Update configurations (5 items): approximately 1 min
    • Deploy + smoke test: approximately 30s
    • Per-domain total: approximately 2 min

What Changed vs What Stayed:

  • Changed: KVM mapping, basepath, routing rules
  • Unchanged: JavaScript routing engine, RaiseFault policy, Statistics Collector
Important Note on iFlow Endpoints:

All 8 iFlows were deployed as stub endpoints (HTTP 200 with mock payload) to validate routing logic. Full iFlow business logic can be developed in parallel with DCRP proxy deployment.

Prerequisites:
  • iFlow endpoint must be deployed and accessible on CPI (e.g., https://tenant.cfapps.eu20.hana.ondemand.com/http/domain/resource)
  • A minimal "Hello World" endpoint suffices for routing validation
  • Production iFlow logic can run in parallel with DCRP setup
Parallel Workflow Example:
  • Day 1: Deploy DCRP proxy (2 min) + stub iFlow → routing validated
  • Day 2-3: Develop full iFlow logic → end-to-end functional

Without an endpoint, DCRP returns HTTP 404, but routing logic remains correct and testable once endpoint is deployed.

8.2.4 - Scalability Projection:

Vertical Scaling (within domain):

  • Add iFlow to existing domain: 30 seconds (KVM update only)
  • Example: Finance starts with 2 iFlows → add 3 more → 30 sec total

Horizontal Scaling (new domains):

  • Deploy new domain with 2 iFlows: 2 minutes
  • Example: Add 6 more domains → 12 minutes

Formula:

Total Time = (N domains × 2 min) + (M iFlows × 30 sec)

Compare to 1:1 Proxy Model:

  • 10 domains × 10 iFlows = 100 proxies × 15 min = 1,500 minutes
  • DCRP: (10 × 2) + (90 × 0.5) = 65 minutes
  • Savings: 96%

IMPORTANT: iFlow Endpoint Prerequisite ( in case of imidiatly test )

Deployment times assume:

  • iFlow endpoint is deployed on CPI (accessible via HTTP)
  • iFlow does NOT need to be fully developed — stub endpoint sufficient
  • Full iFlow logic can be developed in parallel with APIM deployment

Parallel Workflow:

  • Day 1: Deploy DCRP proxy (2 min) + stub iFlow → routing validated ✅
  • Day 2-3: Integration team develops full iFlow logic → end-to-end functional ✅

Without iFlow endpoint: DCRP returns HTTP 404, but routing logic is still correct.

8.2.5 - Scalability Constraints (Transparency)

Scalability at enterprise level depends on multiple factors:

APIM Layer:

  • JavaScript execution overhead
  • KVM lookup performance with large entry sets
  • Concurrent request handling capacity per tenant

CPI Layer:

  • Worker node capacity and concurrent iFlow execution limits
  • Message throughput constraints (MB/hour based on tenant size)
  • Memory allocation per integration flow

The scalability ceiling is determined by both APIM routing capacity AND backend CPI infrastructure, not by DCRP logic alone. Proper sizing of both layers is essential for high-scale deployments.

8.3 - Critical Requirement: Naming Convention Standardization

For DCRP to function optimally at scale, strict naming convention standardization is mandatory across both APIM and CPI:

  • API Proxy naming: Must follow [provider]-[pattern]-[domain]-[subdomain]-[protocol]-api
  • CPI iFlow naming: Must align with KVM pattern httppath:Iflow.Domain.Process.Function
  • KVM entries: Must maintain consistent structure (httppath:iflowname<delimiter>)
  • Governance enforcement: Deviations from the standard will cause routing failures

Without standardized naming conventions enforced through governance, the pattern cannot scale reliably.

This is metadata-driven architecture at enterprise scale: one routing logic, infinite business processes—provided infrastructure and governance support the growth.

8.4 - The Governance Requirement

Critical: Listen up: If your team treats KVM like a free-for-all notepad, this pattern WILL fail.. If teams add entries without following the pattern (httppath:iflowname<delimiter>), the JavaScript routing engine will set routing.failed = true and return structured error messages via the RaiseFault policy.

Best Practices:

  • Establish clear KVM update procedures
  • Use naming conventions documented in this blog
  • Implement peer review for KVM changes
  • Monitor analytics for routing failures (routing.failed metric)
  • Use recommended delimiter (,) for consistency across teams

Bottom Line: After extensive testing of multiple approaches (hardcoding, Target Endpoint properties, dynamic property generation), KVM proved to be the only method that maintains both resilience and scalability at enterprise scale. It's not just a preference—it's an architectural necessity for DCRP to function reliably.

8.5 - The KVM Policy XML: "The Data Source"

This policy retrieves integration metadata from the environment's memory and assigns it to context variables. It must be placed before the JavaScript policy in the Target Pre-Flow to ensure "The Brain" has the necessary data to process routing decisions.

The KVM Policy Configuration in Target Pre-Flow

<KeyValueMapOperations mapIdentifier="cpipackage-nx.sales.domain.process" async="false" continueOnError="false" enabled="true"
	xmlns="http://www.sap.com/apimgmt">
	[XML POLICY THE map Identifier is already in place]
	<Scope>environment</Scope>
</KeyValueMapOperations>

8.6 - The Template Philosophy: Scaling Strategies

Two Strategic Approaches:

Approach 1: Single Global Template

  • One master template for all domains
  • Time per deployment: ~2 minutes
  • Requires 5 manual configurations:
    1. KVM Policy mapIdentifier
    2. Routing Rule condition
    3. Target Endpoint cpi.basepath
    4. API Base Path
    5. (Optional) Security policies
  • Best for: Environments with few domains and low clone frequency

Approach 2: Domain-Specific Templates (RECOMMENDED)

  • One template per business domain
  • Time per deployment: ~30 seconds
  • Requires 1 manual configuration: API Base Path
  • Everything else pre-configured:
    • ✅KVM Policy (correct mapIdentifier)
    • ✅Routing Rules (correct conditions)
    • ✅Target Endpoint (correct cpi.basepath)
    • ✅Security Policies (domain-appropriate)
  • Best for: Enterprise environments with regional/multi-instance deployments

8.7 - When Domain Templates Win:

Regional Instances Global Template Domain Template Savings
5 instances10 min12.5 minBreak-even
10 instances20 min14.5 min5.5 min (27%)
20 instances40 min19.5 min20.5 min (51%)
50 instances100 min34.5 min65.5 min (65%)

Break-even point: 5 instances per domain

ROI: After 5 clones, every additional deployment is 4× faster

Real-World Lesson Learned:

During today's validation, deploying Finance and Procurement resulted in 404 errors.

Root cause: Forgot to update KVM Policy mapIdentifier.

Impact:

  • Sales KVM: cpipackage-nx.sales.domain.process
  • Finance Proxy (cloned from Sales): still pointed to Sales KVM ❌
  • Result: JavaScript looked for /finance/invoices in Sales KVM → not found → 404

Fix time: 2 minutes (update mapIdentifier + redeploy)

With Domain Template: This error cannot happen — mapIdentifier is already correct in template.

9 - The Domain Template Contract:

What's Pre-Configured (Zero Risk of Forgetting):

Proxy Endpoint - PreFlow:
├── Authentication (domain-specific: Basic for Sales, OAuth2 for Finance)
├── Threat Protection (domain-specific policies)
├── Quota (domain-specific limits)
└── Spike Arrest (domain-specific thresholds)

Target Endpoint - PreFlow:
├── KVM Lookup (✅ mapIdentifier already correct)
├── JavaScript - The Brain (✅ universal, no changes)
├── RaiseFault (✅ universal error handling)
├── Statistics Collector (✅ universal analytics)
└── cpi.basepath (✅ already correct for domain)

What You Change (30 seconds):

  • API Base Path: /sales → /sales-eu

That's it. Deploy. Done.

9.1 - Deployment Checklist (Domain Template Strategy):

One-Time Setup (per domain):

  •  Clone Global Template → Domain Template
  •  Configure domain-specific security (Authentication, Threat Protection)
  •  Set KVM mapIdentifier for domain
  •  Set cpi.basepath for domain
  •  Set Routing Rule for domain
  •  Save as Template (do NOT deploy)
  • Time: ~10 minutes per domain

Repeatable Deployment (per instance):

  •  Clone Domain Template
  •  Change API Base Path (/sales → /sales-eu)
  •  Deploy
  •  Test
  • Time: ~30 seconds per instance
IMPORTANT: iFlow Endpoint Availability Requirement

Critical Pre-Requisite for DCRP Validation:

All deployment times stated above (2 minutes for Global Template, 30 seconds for Domain Template) assume that:

  1. The iFlow endpoint is already deployed and available on CPI
  2. The iFlow HTTP endpoint is accessible (e.g., https://<tenant>.cfapps.eu20.hana.ondemand.com/http/<domain>/<resource>)
  3. The iFlow does NOT need to be fully developed — a minimal "Hello World" or echo endpoint is sufficient for routing validation

What This Means in Practice:

  •  For connectivity validation: Only the iFlow endpoint needs to exist
  • For DCRP routing tests: The iFlow can return a simple HTTP 200 with mock payload
  • For production: Full iFlow logic (mappings, transformations, backend calls) can be developed in parallel

Why This Matters:

In enterprise environments, API Proxy deployment and iFlow development often happen in parallel:

  • API Team: Deploys DCRP proxy in 30 seconds (using Domain Template)
  • Integration Team: Develops full iFlow logic over days/weeks
  • Validation Team: Can test routing immediately with stub iFlow

Example: Parallel Development Workflow

Day API Team Integration Team Status
Day 1Deploy Finance proxy (30 sec)Create stub iFlow endpoint✅Routing validated
Day 2Deploy Sales proxy (30 sec)Add data mapping logic✅Routing still works
Day 3Deploy Logistics proxy (30 sec)Integrate backend systems✅End-to-end functional

Without iFlow endpoint available: DCRP proxy will return HTTP 404 or connection timeout — but the APIM routing itself is still correct.

Bottom line:

  • DCRP deployment time = 30 seconds
  • iFlow endpoint availability = prerequisite (can be stub)
  • Full iFlow development = parallel track (does not block APIM deployment)

9.2 JavaScript Policy – Dissecting the DCRP Routing Engine

Now we arrive at the heart of the Viana Pattern – the JavaScript policy that transforms static gateway infrastructure into a dynamic, metadata-driven routing engine. This function is where the "magic" happens, famously noted - Let's demystify this magic.

"Any sufficiently advanced technology is indistinguishable from magic."  - Arthur C. Clarke

And trust me, when you see this routing engine in action, it DOES feel like magic. But it's not. It's just good architecture with disciplined execution. Let me show you how it works.

Figure 8 - The Commander: Executing the Brain

9.3- The Complete JavaScript Code

/**
 * ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
 * DCRP - Domain-Centric Routing Pattern
 * The Commander's Brain (Viana Pattern)
 * Version: 1.3 - Production Ready with Flexible Delimiters
 * ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
 * 
 * CAPABILITIES:
 * - Dynamic iFlow resolution via metadata (KVM)
 * - Path parameter preservation
 * - Query string integrity across gateways
 * - Governance header injection (X-SenderId, X-CorrelationId)
 * - Analytics variable collection
 * - Fault triggers for error scenarios
 * - Zero-code deployment for new integrations (metadata-only)
 * 
 * Author: Viana, SAP BTP Integration Suite Expert
 * Pattern: DCRP (Domain-Centric Routing Pattern)
 * ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
 */

function resolveDcprRouting() {
    
    // SECTION 1: CONTEXT EXTRACTION
    var targetBase = context.getVariable("target.cpi.host");
    var cpiBasePath = context.getVariable("target.cpi.basepath");
    var kvmMapIflowString = context.getVariable("iflowname");
    var rawSuffix = context.getVariable("proxy.pathsuffix") || "";
    
    // Governance headers
    var senderId = context.getVariable("request.header.X-SenderId") || "UnknownSender";
    var corId = context.getVariable("request.header.X-CorrelationId") || "NoCorrID";
    
    var iflowName = "UnknownIflow";
    var resourceName = "";
    var matchedKey = "";

    // SECTION 2: FLEXIBLE DELIMITER DETECTION & KVM PARSING
    // Supported delimiters (colon reserved for path:iflow separation)
    var allowedDelimiters = [',', '/', '\\', '[', ']', '(', ')', '-', ';', '|', '_'];
    
    var detectedDelimiter = ','; // default
    
    // Detect which delimiter is used in the KVM string
    if (kvmMapIflowString) {
        for (var d = 0; d < allowedDelimiters.length; d++) {
            if (kvmMapIflowString.indexOf(allowedDelimiters[d]) !== -1) {
                detectedDelimiter = allowedDelimiters[d];
                break;
            }
        }
        
        // Split by detected delimiter and parse routes
        var routes = kvmMapIflowString.split(detectedDelimiter).map(function(entry) {
            return entry.trim();
        });
        
        // Sort routes by pathKey length (longest first for specificity)
        var parsedRoutes = [];
        for (var i = 0; i < routes.length; i++) {
            if (routes[i] === "") continue;
            
            var splitResult = routes[i].split(':').map(function(part) {
                return part.trim();
            });
            
            if (splitResult.length >= 2) {
                parsedRoutes.push({
                    pathKey: splitResult[0],
                    iflowName: splitResult[1]
                });
            }
        }
        
        // Sort by length (longest pathKey first to match most specific routes)
        parsedRoutes.sort(function(a, b) {
            return b.pathKey.length - a.pathKey.length;
        });
        
        // Find matching route
        for (var r = 0; r < parsedRoutes.length; r++) {
            var pathKey = parsedRoutes[r].pathKey;
            if (rawSuffix.indexOf("/" + pathKey) !== -1) {
                matchedKey = pathKey;
                resourceName = "/" + pathKey;
                iflowName = parsedRoutes[r].iflowName;
                break;
            }
        }
    }

    // SECTION 3: VALIDATION & FAULT DETECTION
    if (!targetBase || targetBase === "") {
        context.setVariable("routing.failed", "true");
        context.setVariable("routing.error.cause", "DCRP_CONFIG_ERROR: target.cpi.host is not configured");
        return;
    }
    
    if (!cpiBasePath || cpiBasePath === "") {
        context.setVariable("routing.failed", "true");
        context.setVariable("routing.error.cause", "DCRP_CONFIG_ERROR: target.cpi.basepath is not configured");
        return;
    }
    
    if (!senderId || senderId === "UnknownSender") {
        context.setVariable("routing.failed", "true");
        context.setVariable("routing.error.cause", "DCRP_GOVERNANCE_ERROR: X-SenderId header is missing or empty");
        return;
    }
    
    if (!corId || corId === "NoCorrID") {
        context.setVariable("routing.failed", "true");
        context.setVariable("routing.error.cause", "DCRP_GOVERNANCE_ERROR: X-CorrelationId header is missing or empty");
        return;
    }
    
    if (!kvmMapIflowString || kvmMapIflowString === "") {
        context.setVariable("routing.failed", "true");
        context.setVariable("routing.error.cause", "DCRP_KVM_ERROR: iflowname KVM entry is empty");
        return;
    }
    
    if (iflowName === "UnknownIflow" || resourceName === "") {
        context.setVariable("routing.failed", "true");
        context.setVariable("routing.error.cause", "DCRP_ROUTE_NOT_FOUND: No iFlow mapping found for path: " + rawSuffix);
        return;
    }

    // SECTION 4: GOVERNANCE HEADER INJECTION
    context.targetRequest.headers['X-SenderId'] = senderId;
    context.targetRequest.headers['X-CorrelationId'] = corId;

    // SECTION 5: DYNAMIC URL CONSTRUCTION
    var finalUrl = targetBase.toString() + cpiBasePath.toString() + resourceName;
    
    // Extract extra path after matched resource
    if (matchedKey !== "") {
        var pathSegments = rawSuffix.split("/" + matchedKey);
        if (pathSegments.length > 1 && pathSegments[1] !== "" && pathSegments[1] !== "/") {
            var extraPath = pathSegments[1].split("?")[0];
            finalUrl = finalUrl + extraPath;
        }
    }
    
    // Preserve query string
    var queryString = context.getVariable("request.querystring");
    if (queryString) {
        finalUrl = finalUrl + "?" + queryString;
    }

    // SECTION 6: TARGET CONFIGURATION
    context.setVariable("target.copy.pathsuffix", false);
    context.setVariable("target.copy.queryparams", false);
    context.setVariable("target.url", finalUrl);

    // SECTION 7: ANALYTICS VARIABLE INJECTION
    context.setVariable("custom.idsender", senderId);
    context.setVariable("custom.idiflow", iflowName);
    context.setVariable("custom.idcorrelation", corId);
    
    context.setVariable("routing.failed", "false");
}

9.4 - How the Routing Engine Works

The JavaScript function executes seven critical operations in sequence:

Section 1: Context Extraction Retrieves six essential variables from the Apigee runtime context:

  • target.cpi.host: CPI tenant base URL
  • target.cpi.basepath: CPI path prefix (e.g., /http/orders)
  • iflowname: KVM string containing route mappings
  • proxy.pathsuffix: Path after proxy base (e.g., /inbound/12345)
  • X-SenderId: Governance tracking header
  • X-CorrelationId: End-to-end transaction tracing header

Section 2: Flexible Delimiter Detection and KVM Parsing The routing engine automatically detects which delimiter is used in the KVM string. Supported delimiters include: comma, forward slash, backslash, brackets, parentheses, hyphen, semicolon, pipe, and underscore. The colon is reserved for separating path from iFlow name.

The algorithm splits the KVM string by the detected delimiter, parses each entry into pathKey and iflowName pairs, then sorts routes by pathKey length (longest first) to ensure most specific routes match first. This prevents ambiguous routing—for example, /processing/invoices matches before /invoices.

Example KVM string:

inbound:SO.01.Iflow.Sales.Order.Process.Inbound,
tracking:SO.02.Iflow.Sales.Order.Tracking.Provider,
detail:SO.04.Iflow.Sales.Order.Detail.Provider

Alternative formatting with pipe delimiter:

inbound:SO.01.Iflow.Sales.Order.Process.Inbound |
tracking:SO.02.Iflow.Sales.Order.Tracking.Provider |
detail:SO.04.Iflow.Sales.Order.Detail.Provider

Performance: Linear complexity O(n) where n equals number of routes. At 20 routes per KVM, worst-case execution completes in under 1 millisecond. For deployments exceeding 100 routes per domain, consider implementing hash map lookup for O(1) performance.

Section 3: Validation and Fault Detection Before constructing the target URL, the function validates all required configurations:

  • Configuration errors: Missing target.cpi.host or cpi.basepath
  • Governance errors: Missing X-SenderId or X-CorrelationId headers
  • KVM errors: Empty iflowname entry
  • Routing errors: No matching path found in KVM

When validation fails, the function sets routing.failed = true and populates routing.error.cause with a descriptive error message. The RaiseFault policy (configured in Target Endpoint) catches these failures and returns structured error responses to consumers.

Section 4: Governance Header Injection Injects X-SenderId, X-CorrelationId and other parameters to be traced in SAP ALM into the target request headers. These headers enable:

  • Multi-tenant identification: Filter CPI logs by calling system
  • End-to-end tracing: Link APIM logs to CPI logs to backend system logs
  • Incident triage: Reduce mean-time-to-resolution by 60-80% compared to untraced requests

Section 5: Dynamic URL Construction Constructs the final target URL while preserving path parameters and query strings.

Example transformation:

Public API call:

GET https://apim/sales/orders/inbound/PO-2024-001234?status=COMPLETED

Constructed target URL:

https://tenant.cpi.com/http/orders/inbound/PO-2024-001234?status=COMPLETE

URL components:

  • targetBase: https://tenant.cpi.com
  • cpiBasePath: /http/orders
  • resourceName: /inbound (matched from KVM)
  • extraPath: /PO-2024-001234 (extracted after resourceName)
  • queryString: ?status=COMPLETED (preserved from original request)

The /http/ prefix is required by CPI's HTTP adapter to identify HTTP-based iFlow endpoints.

Section 6: Target Configuration Disables Apigee's automatic path and query parameter copying by setting:

  • target.copy.pathsuffix = false
  • target.copy.queryparams = false

Why this matters: The SAP APIM by default appends proxy.pathsuffix and query parameters to target URLs. Without disabling auto-copy, the final request would contain duplicate path segments and query parameters, resulting in malformed URLs and 404 errors.

With auto-copy disabled, the precisely constructed URL matches our routing intent.

Sets target.url to the dynamically constructed finalUrl. Once this variable is set, Apigee's target flow invokes the CPI iFlow using this endpoint.

Section 7: Analytics Variable Injection Populates custom analytics variables for the Statistics Collector policy:

  • custom.idsender: Sender identification
  • custom.idiflow: Target iFlow name
  • custom.idcorrelation: Correlation identifier

These variables enable:

  • Dashboard visualization: Traffic distribution across iFlows
  • Performance analysis: Response times by CPI path
  • Capacity planning: Request volume trends per iFlow
  • Debugging: Quick identification of routing decisions

9.5 - Chapter 9 Summary

The JavaScript routing engine performs seven operations that transform a generic API request into a precisely targeted CPI iFlow invocation:

  1. Extracts execution context from Apigee variables
  2. Detects delimiter and parses KVM metadata to match paths to iFlow names
  3. Validates configuration and sets fault triggers for error scenarios
  4. Injects governance headers for end-to-end traceability
  5. Constructs target URLs while preserving path parameters and query strings
  6. Disables Apigee's auto-copy to prevent URL duplication
  7. Sets analytics variables for observability

The routing algorithm uses linear search (O(n) complexity) which is suitable for typical enterprise deployments with 10-50 routes per domain. For environments requiring 100+ routes per domain, consider domain subdivision or hash map optimization.

10 -Statistics Collector – Capturing Business Intelligence

While the JavaScript "Brain" executes the routing logic, we need visibility into what's happening in production. The Statistics Collector policy transforms technical execution into actionable business intelligence by capturing custom dimensions into SAP API Management's analytics database.

Purpose: Record routing metadata (iFlow name, package name, domain process, sender ID, correlation ID) for real-time monitoring, trend analysis, and capacity planning.

Details insade spoiler:

Spoiler
<!--Specify Stats collector policy to add custom dimensions and measures -->
<StatisticsCollector async='false' continueOnError='false' enabled='true'
	xmlns='http://www.sap.com/apimgmt'>
	<Statistics>
		<!-- prepopulated template -->
		<!-- Uncomment Statistic which needs to be collected and define reference variable-->
		<Statistic name='custom_idcorrelation' ref='idcorrelation' type='string'>0</Statistic>
		<Statistic name='custom_idflow' ref='idiflow' type='string'>0</Statistic>
		<Statistic name='custom_idpackage' ref='idpackage' type='string'>0</Statistic>
		<Statistic name='custom_idsaprocess' ref='idsapprocess' type='string'>0</Statistic>
		<Statistic name='custom_idsender' ref='idsender' type='string'>0</Statistic>
	</Statistics>
</StatisticsCollector>

Before you think about scaling this proposal I'm offering, pause and prepare. This is not a "quick win" implementation. You need to:

  • Align your team – Developers, architects, and platform owners must understand the paradigm shift
  • Educate stakeholders – Business sponsors need to grasp why consolidation beats proliferation
  • Establish governance – Define clear ownership, approval workflows, and change management processes
  • Design naming conventions – The [provider]-[pattern]-[domain]-[subdomain]-[protocol]-api structure is non-negotiable
  • Structure a PoC first – Validate with 2-3 iFlows in a sandbox before scaling to production

Choose wisely – because once you commit to an architecture, changing course is messy, and trust me, tomato sauce stains are hard to get out. 

Conclusion

As an SAP BTP Integration Suite Expert, I've seen the struggle. DCRP is the governance layer SAP APIM needs but doesn't provide out-of-the-box.

Benefits of the DCRP:

✅ Removes Complexity: Takes the technical OData burden off the consumer
✅ Maintains Semantic Integrity: Separates the Business Domain (APIM) from the Technical Orchestration (CPI)
✅ Future Proof: Allows consumer contracts to remain unchanged regardless of backend service or version changes

Using the DCRP in your landscape, you will create a scalable, semantic, and governed gateway for the entire SAP ecosystem—not just a collection of endpoints.

Figure 9 - The Commander: Discipline Over Chaos

If you made it this far, you're either serious about fixing your SAP landscape or you really like long technical reads. Either way, let's connect.

What's next? 

You should not miss Part II—it's the crucial piece to finally solve the "CPI Package Sprawl." Based on deep analysis, attempting to solve "Endpoint Sprawl" with a central CPI iFlow "Package Router" creates an unmanageable "Frankenstein iFlow"—so keep it clean with a 1:1 endpoint relationship.


"In architecture, as in the field, discipline is not about restriction; it is about creating the boundaries that allow your forces to scale without chaos." — The Commander - Ricardo "Viana"

See you in Part II ! 

If this article helped you, please:

  • Like and comment with your SAP integration experience
  • Share with your integration community
  • Reach out if you're implementing DCRP in your landscape

Connect with me:

  • LinkedIn: The Commander - Viana
  • Domain-Centric Routing Pattern (DCRP) - Slideck

Kind Regards,

SAP BTP Integration Suite Expert - Viana

entry.title: Re: Error During TO Creation--Available quantity in bin is zero(Message no. L3019)
entry.content: Hi, it is possible that you have discrapeancies between LQUA and LQUAB tables. Please check the correctif report RLTLQUAB

entry.title: Retrieving Users and Groups via SCIM: Integrating SAP Ariba with SAP Cloud Identity Services
entry.content:
SAP Ariba SCIM API

The SAP Ariba SCIM API is a REST API based on the SCIM (System for Cross-domain Identity Management) 2.0 specification. It is intended for use as an interface between SAP Ariba cloud solutions and SAP Cloud Identity Services to transfer user information as part of user management.

Prerequisites

To call the methods of this SAP Ariba SCIM API you must have SAP Ariba Procurement or Strategic Sourcing Realms.

ACCESS SAP Ariba APIs

1. Access SAP Ariba APIs : https://developer.ariba.com/api/home

2. Select the right Data Center where your SAP Ariba Realms located, in my case is Japan Data Center:

3. To find the API Specs, click Discover, fill the right solution, Procurement or Strategic Sourcing, search the page with SCIM API for User and Group Master Data and click.

4. Now we will request SCIM API. Go to Manage, click + to Create a New Application.

5. Fill necessary information and click submit:

6. Application created, you can click Action and then click Request API Access, then select the SCIM API and select SAP Ariba Realms that you want to retrieve data from. Then click Submit.

7. Screen shows as below and auto-Approval will happen in about 1 day:

 

8. Once approved, click Actions and click Generate Auth Secret. Note down them and will be used in API.

CALL SAP Ariba SCIM API

1. My personal preference is to test post and get in PowerShell and once it works ok, configure in Postman or Bruno to have a more filtered or formatted view of the result. Now open PowerShell:

2. Type below into powershell to post and get the Access_Token:

3. Enter and you will find the necessary access token and token type as below, note them down:

4. Now use the access token above to type below command, enter and all users will be listed as below:

Now all necessary info to call SCIM and retrieve user and group data is ready. 

CALL SAP Ariba SCIM API in Bruno

1. Change to Bruno UI, create a new request, select Type as HTTP , fill Request Name , select URL  with POST and fill the URL, then click Create.

2. Go to Headers, configure Content-Type as application/x-www-form-urlencoded.

3. Then go to params, configure below 3 attributes:

grant_type : client_credentials

client_id : Oauth Client Id

client_secret : Oauth Secret Generated

4. Run the request and note down the access_token:

 

5. Create a new request, select Type as HTTP , fill Request Name , select URL  with GET and URL, then click Create. 

6. Go to Headers, fill below four attributes:

Authorization : <token_type> <access_token>

apiKey : Application API Key

x-anId : Realm ANID *for test with -T

Accept : application/scim+json

7. Run the request, you can see more formatted data as below:

8. Based on the specs below, you can search more data with filters etc.

For example, you want to get User with email address equals below:

9. You can also retrieve all group data as below:

 

Identity Directory API (CIS - IdDS)

Manage users, groups and custom schemas in the cloud. Help Portal : https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-identity-services/cloud-identity-services/identity-directory-scim-rest-api

Prerequisites

To call the methods of this SCIM REST API you must have a system as administrator with an assigned Manage Users role. 

API SPECS

Please find detailed API info from here : https://api.sap.com/api/IdDS_SCIM/overview 

 

ACCESS Identity Directory API:

Make sure you are Administrator User in CIS - idDS:

1. Administrator can login to CIS via link : https://<Tenant ID>.accounts.ondemand.com/admin/

2. To check other Administrators, click and select as below:

 

3. Call API via PowerShell by typing as below, click enter and you will see all users.

-u UserID:Password

-H "Accept: application/scim+json" `

-H "Content-Type: application/scim+json"

4. Now use Bruno to get better formatted result. Create a Request as below:

5. Clich Auth, then select Basic Auth from dropdown list, Fill UserName and password with your Administrator(User) email address and password.

6. Click Headers, configure Accept and Content-Type as application/scim+json.

7. Execute and all users of CIS(IdDS) retrieved as below:

8. Add filter based on user Login Name, execute then you can see the user has been extracted:

9. You can also retrieve group data by filter as below:

 

 

Summary

This blog walked through how to retrieve user and group master data using SCIM 2.0 APIs from both SAP Ariba and SAP Cloud Identity Services (Identity Directory / IdDS)—a foundational requirement for user provisioning and synchronization scenarios.

Instead of introducing the full functionality of Identity Provisioning Service (IPS), the focus was placed on the practical API calls that IPS relies on internally: how to access the API specifications, request API access, authenticate, and retrieve users and groups using SCIM-compliant endpoints. Both PowerShell and Bruno were used to demonstrate how to call these APIs and inspect responses in a structured and readable way.

By understanding how SAP Ariba and Identity Directory expose identity data through SCIM—along with supported filters, attributes, and authentication mechanisms—you gain deeper insight into IPS properties, mappings, and transformations. This knowledge is especially critical when enabling SAP Ariba Joule, where accurate user and group synchronization across SAP Ariba, SAP Cloud Identity Services, and SAP Build Work Zone is essential.

In short, mastering these SCIM APIs is not just about calling endpoints—it is about building a solid foundation for reliable identity integration in SAP’s AI-driven and Business AI–focused landscape.

entry.title: Re: Utilizing SAP BTP Integration Suite for Integration with SAP Sales and Service Cloud V2
entry.content:
Hi @kishorekumar_p , nice blog.

What about the limit of $skip not more than 10000? It seems that the sum between $top and $skip must be under 10000.

https://community.sap.com/t5/crm-and-cx-blog-posts-by-members/connecting-sap-sales-cloud-v2-to-a-standalone-sap-analytics-cloud-a/ba-p/14310439

My Idea is to use this kind of query: 

?$count=true&$top=999&$orderby=displayId&$filter=displayId gt '<lastDisplayId>'

  • Count to check if is greater then top limit (999)
  • orderby to order the displayId value from lower to higher
  • If remaining Count > 999, save the last displayId in your payload and use it to filter only records with displayId gt '<lastDisplayId>'

Again, while remaining Count > 999, repeat the loop. Else, do the last call and exit the loop.

Let me know if you have better ideas 🙂

Regards,

Francesco

entry.title: Need inputs on how to publish salary recommendation to multiple pay components
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

Actually, I am looking for some guidance on publishing multiple entries to recurring pay component portlet.

Here is my requirement.

For ex: In Asia, the salary has several components like HRA (House Rent Allowance), DA (Daily Allowance) etc. And these buckets are of either % based or amount based. For example, if base salary is 100,000, the HRA and DA can be 20% and 10% of the base salary or can be maintained as 20000 and 15000 respectively.

When we do focal cycle, we generally enter recommendation based on the total salary and not on individual components. In the above case, if we give 3% increase of 100,000 (base salary), while publishing it has to publish 20.6% and 10.3% respectively or 20,600 and 15,450 respectively.

How can we achieve this? Any other customer had this requirement and how was this implemented?

Appreciate any guidance on this.

Thank you.

entry.title: Scalable Report Broadcasting in SAP Analytics Cloud: Replacing BusinessObjects Scheduling with APOS
entry.content:
I recently delivered a presentation at the SAP Platform and Data Summit in Toronto on our company's data fabric and analytics  modernization journey from SAP BusinessObjects and a HANA sidecar to SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere. My presentation focused on our efforts to replace our BusinessObjects scheduling and broadcasting workflows in SAC.

With the help of our implementation partner SimpleFi Solutions and SAP, the deployment of SAC and Datasphere went very smoothly, but when it came to replicating our BusinessObjects scheduling and broadcasting workflows, we ran into some serious complications.

Landscape

At the start of this process, our landscape looked like this:

  • Our system of record was ECC.
  • We used an SAP Landscape Transformation (SLT) server to replicate data to a HANA sidecar.
  • A BusinessObjects universe was built on top of calculation views.
  • We also used BusinessObjects Data Service for some weekend jobs, inventory jobs, month-end jobs, etc.
  • An SFTP server handled files.
  • We used BI Launchpad for reporting (over 2,000 reports, with no audit system).
  • Our e-commerce team used (and continues to use), the DOMO dashboarding and visualization tool, and data was fed to DOMO from BusinessObjects system. We set up an instance schedule which would feed that data to DOMO.
  • Distributors and sales reps logged in to our B2B website to see inventory and order. The inventory files came in from the BusinessObjects system.

In late 2024, we started our modernization journey, driven by:

  • Cost: How do we save money by reducing the number of systems in play?
  • New functionality: How do we leverage new features in the cloud-based system?
  • BusinessObjects retirement: We wanted to get out of BusinessObjects and deploy SAP Analytics Cloud.

SAP made a very strong case for moving our analytics to the cloud, using Datasphere to replace our HANA sidecar, and SAC to replace BusinessObjects. We moved SAP ECC to ECC on RISE. It was a relatively smooth move.

The modernized data fabric and analytics landscape now looks like this:

Critical Report Challenges

SAP cloud-based data fabric and analytics held great promise, but we hit obstacles with SAP Analytics Cloud Publications. These obstacles are common to cloud-based BI & analytics platforms, but we needed a strategy to replicate our report distribution workflows from BusinessObjects.

SAC Publications limitations:

  • Inability to handle a high volume of reports
  • Inability to handle a high volume of jobs
  • Inability to process large data sets
  • Restrictions on external recipients
  • Inconsistencies in Excel formatting

The new landscape includes APOS Publisher for Cloud, which we implemented to address our broadcasting needs.

We had a number of use cases we needed to replicate in the new landscape, but in summary, these were the requirements:

  • Timely personalized reports to Sales reps (many of whom were external to the company)
  • Large data extracts of up to 200,000 rows
  • Regular updates to DOMO for data integration and analysis
  • Hourly updates via SFTP for operational needs

To meet these use cases, we needed “expanded broadcasting capabilities beyond those available in SAC.

Report Broadcasting with APOS Publisher for Cloud

We assessed our broadcasting needs and reached out to our partner SimpleFi Solutions and to our SAP account executive, who introduced us to APOS. After initial consultations with APOS solution consultants, we entered into a proof of concept. The proof of concept demonstrated that the APOS Publisher for Cloud solution could meet our needs with regard to complexity, volume, formatting, personalization and security.

With the help of APOS, we were able to get to a successful go-live with:

  • All use case requirements met
  • A long-term, scalable report broadcasting solution
  • Simplified scheduling and broadcasting administration
  • Satisfied sales reps and C-level execs

Use Cases

Publisher for Cloud allowed us to fulfill use cases that were not possible with SAC Publications. Here are a few:

  • Use Case #1: Send Open Orders Workbooks to Reps and Account Service Teams
  • Use Case #2: Summarized Data (e.g., Flash Report)
  • Use Case #3: Data Feed to External Reporting System

Use Case #1: Send Open Orders Workbooks to Reps and Account Service Teams

The Open Orders report is a very large report in an Excel workbook. It is extremely important that reps get the report first thing in the morning for their daily activities, and that each rep get only the data to which they are entitled, and in the Excel format to which they are accustomed.

Requirements:

  • Many reps are external 
  • Reps are accustomed and committed to having these reports delivered to their inboxes
  • Formatted Excel

In Publisher for Cloud, we set up a bursting definition with:

  • Source: SAC story
  • Data Source: Excel spreadsheet with filters and email recipients (Example - Sales Rep1 - Email to john.doe@xyz.com)
  • Format: Pre-defined Excel spreadsheet

Use Case #2: Summarized Data (e.g., Flash Report)

The Summarized Data report goes to C-level executives first thing in the morning, summarizing data from multiple sources (billing information, inventory, MTD data, YTD data, etc.), delivered in a multi-tab Excel workbook.

  • Delivery must be timely and without fail
  • Formatted Excel
  • Executives are accustomed and committed to having these reports delivered to their inboxes

In Publisher for Cloud, we set up a bursting definition with:

  • Source: SAC story
  • Data Source: Excel spreadsheet with email recipients
  • Format: Multiple pages pointing to different table widgets in the story

Use Case #3: Data Feed to External Reporting System

The data feed to an external reporting system is used to create a near real-time dashboard for management. For this feed, we set up an SAC story and needed to generate and send a report with a pre-determined frequency. Timely generation and execution of this report was critical.

In Publisher for Cloud, we set up a bursting definition with:

  • Source: SAC story
  • Data Source: raw data in CSV or Excel format
  • Format: Pre-defined Excel spreadsheet

Outcomes

The three use cases described above were critical to a successful transformation project, and Publisher for Cloud was instrumental in overcoming the limitations we found in SAC Publications. Publisher for Cloud allowed us to replicate the report distribution workflows that SAP BusinessObjects handled so successfully. With Publisher for Cloud, we achieved:

  • High volume reporting
  • Ability to process large data sets
  • No restrictions on external recipients
  • Consistent Excel formatting

The successful deployment of SAC and Publisher for Cloud means we are free to consider many new use cases to transform our business processes, and Publisher for Cloud’s recent integration with agentic AI opens up endless possibilities for improved analytics workflows

entry.title: BIB Replication: Employee Central to S/4
entry.content:
Replicating employee data from SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central (EC) to SAP S/4HANA is a foundational step for organizations operating in a Core Hybrid HCM landscape. In this setup, Employee Central serves as the system of record for HR master data, while S/4HANA supports downstream processes like payroll, time management, and organizational management.

This blog provides a comprehensive, easy-to-follow guide to help you understand the key components, architecture, implementation steps, and best practices involved in setting up Employee Central to S/4HANA replication.

Integration Architecture Overview

The replication framework consists of three major components working together:

       SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central

  • Acts as the master data for core HR data and organizational assignments.
  • Employs the Compound Employee API to extract employee data changes.
  • Provides country-specific and global employee information.

      SAP Cloud Integration (Middleware)

  • Ensures technical connectivity between EC and S/4HANA.
  • Handles authentication, session handling, transformations, and protocol conversion.
  • Uses standard SAP-delivered integration packages for:
    • Employee Central → ERP / S/4HANA integration
    • Organizational assignment replication
    • Employee data replication mapping

       SAP S/4HANA

  • Receives employee data via web services.
  • Uses the Business Integration Builder (BIB) to:
    • Map fields
    • Transform values
    • Process and store the replicated data

Key Replication Components

Employee Data Types Replicated

  • Employee Master Data (employment and compensation)
  • Organizational Assignments (department, position, reporting lines)
  • Country-Specific Data (localized fields depending on region)

Supported Infotypes

Commonly replicated infotypes include:

  • 0000 – Actions
  • 0001 – Organizational Assignment
  • 0002 – Personal Data
  • 0006 – Addresses
  • 0007 – Planned Working Time
  • 0008 – Basic Pay
  • 0105 – Communication
  • 0709 – Person ID

Additional Infotypes via BADIs:

  • 0169 – Savings Plan
  • 0171 – General Benefits
  • Others depending on local requirements

 Implementation Steps

Prerequisites:

System Requirements

  • SAP S/4HANA (On-Premises)
  • ECS4HCM add-on installed
  • Required Support Packs & SAP Notes

Project Team

  • Employee Central and S/4 HCM Consultants
  • Middleware (SAP Integration Suite) Consultants
  • Replication Consultants

Configuration Process

Step 1: System Preparation

  • Install required add-ons (ECS4HCM)
  • Configure middleware connections
  • Set up authentication and communication arrangements

Step 2: Data Mapping Using BIB

  • Import EC metadata (XML) into BIB
  • Define field and value mapping rules
  • Set up:
    • Transformation templates
    • Value mapping groups
    • Mapping groups per entity

BIB ensures that EC fields correctly map to S/4HANA infotypes.

Step 3: Replication Setup

  • Define replication schedules (push/pull)
  • Configure the staging area for employee data
  • Setup delta replication for ongoing synchronization
  • Apply filtering criteria if replicating partial data

 

 

Replication Methods

       Pull Replication

  • S/4HANA retrieves data from EC on a scheduled basis.
  • Background jobs manage replication.
  • Staging tables (e.g., PAOC* tables) temporarily store data.

       Push Replication

  • EC sends data to S/4HANA immediately when changes occur.
  • Supports near real-time synchronization.
  • Reduces dependency on batch jobs.

Push replication is preferred for organizations moving toward real-time HR operations.

 

Best Practices for Successful Replication

Data Synchronization

  • Always replicate organizational objects before employee data.
  • Use the standard Employee Replication Interface
  • Run an initial load to establish a proper structural hierarchy.

 

Error Prevention

  • Align validity dates across EC and S/4HANA.
  • Ensure no missing cost centers or org units.
  • Validate all required value mappings in advance.
  • Test with a small population before a mass load.

 

Monitoring & Maintenance

  • Regularly check replication job logs.
  • Monitor Open and Pending staging requests.
  • Maintain ID mappings (Employee IDs, Position IDs, Org IDs).
  • Monitor performance and resolve staging area bottlenecks.

 

Other Considerations

       Data Consistency

  • Ensure effective-dated data aligns between EC and S/4HANA.
  • Handle split records (job changes, pay changes) carefully.
  • Maintain correct referential integrity between employee and organizational objects.

      Security & Compliance

  • Use role-based permissions to secure data.
  • Enable secure authentication between EC and middleware.
  • Ensure data protection compliance (GDPR, regional laws).

      Performance Optimization

  • Adjust middleware packet sizes.
  • Schedule replication during low-system utilization.

A successful Employee Central to S/4HANA replication setup enables companies to:

  • Use Employee Central for modern HR data management
  • Retain S/4HANA for operational processes like payroll and time evaluation
  • Achieve seamless hybrid HCM integration

 

 Replication Approach:

    PULL Replication:

     Better Control and Reliability thru Scheduled processing and background jobs, staging area utilizations, Time out management can be adjusted based on system capacity, Error management thru retry mechanism and better trouble shooting capabilities with detailed error logging and prevents system overload during mass data and reduces dependency issues with the system.

    PUSH Replication:

     Used for the primary critical selective events only – Hire, Re-hire and termination thru Intelligent Service center but has technical limitations with session management problems, data consistency issues and maintenance overhead including complex troubleshooting with request stuck with “Open” or “Pending” status and needed manual intervention, limited extensibility to implement custom BADI for additional infotypes and solutions, outdated replication requests can be processed incorrectly.

 

Best Practice and Better Options:

  • Use Pull replication as primary method for regular data synchronization and enable push replication for critical events (Hire, and Termination if really needed).
  • Pull Config optimization by replication interval to be at every 15 mins, time out adjustment based on system capacity, page size of employees and use single transformation template group.
  • Implement Comprehensive monitoring through SRTUTIL, SLG1 and DRM.
  • Configure the appropriate FTSD date to reduce data volume and go for top of stack and use server-side paging in SF Provisioning.

 

Recommended Approach:

Pull replication is strongly recommended as the primary integration method because it provides:

  • Better system stability and performance
  • Superior error handling and recovery
  • More predictable resource utilization
  • Easier maintenance and troubleshooting

Push replication should only be used as a supplementary method for immediate replication of critical events (hire, rehire, termination) when business requirements demand real-time processing and not recommended for large populations.
